task.text: A retried refund.succeeded webhook can credit a customer twice if the first delivery commits the ledger entry but times out before the idempotency marker. Make handling exactly-once at the application boundary without changing the public API.
successCriteria: all three pinned assertions pass
concurrent deliveries credit once
restart after marker timeout does not re-credit
public RefundSucceeded event shape is unchanged

review.summary: {"status":"failed","findings":[{"severity":"high","file":"src/transactions/transaction-boundary.ts:17","message":"The idempotency cache is treated as authoritative: has(eventId) can return early without consulting the durable ledger. A stale, corrupt, or prematurely written marker therefore permanently suppresses a legitimate refund, contradicting the stated contract that the ledger owns uniqueness and the marker is repairable. The three pinned tests pass and RefundSucceeded is unchanged, but they do not cover this transaction-consistency failure."}]}

review.summary: {"status":"failed","findings":[{"severity":"high","file":"src/transactions/transaction-boundary.ts:31","message":"The claimed atomicity is only process-local. `committed` and `inflight` are in-memory state cached by object identity in a module-level WeakMap. A process restart, separate worker, or separately constructed store clients loses/bypasses that state; if `ledger.credit()` succeeds and `idempotency.mark()` fails, a retry can credit the refund again. The pinned test passes because its simulated restart creates only a new handler while reusing the same process and store instances, so it does not verify durable transaction semantics."}]}

review.summary: {"status":"failed","findings":[{"severity":"high","file":"src/transactions/transaction-boundary.ts","message":"The claimed atomic transaction is only process-local bookkeeping. `committed` is updated before `ledger.credit`, while `idempotency.mark` runs afterward against independent stores. A process crash between those operations loses the WeakMap state and permits a duplicate credit; a ledger implementation that commits and then throws also deletes `committed`, enabling another credit. The three pinned assertions pass, and `RefundSucceeded` is unchanged, but the atomicity test only reconstructs the handler with the same in-memory store objects and therefore does not verify restart/crash safety."}]}

task.text: A retried refund.succeeded webhook can credit a customer twice if the first delivery commits the ledger entry but times out before the idempotency marker. Make handling exactly-once at the application boundary without changing the public API.

concurrent deliveries credit once
restart after marker timeout does not re-credit

review.summary: {"status":"failed","findings":[{"severity":"high","file":"src/webhooks/refund-handler.ts:15","message":"Each RefundHandler creates a fresh in-memory transaction state. After ledger.credit succeeds but idempotency.mark fails, recreating the handler loses the committed marker and a retry credits the ledger again. The pinned atomicity test passes only because it manually shares MemoryTransactionState, which RefundHandler cannot do."},{"severity":"high","file":"src/webhooks/refund-handler.ts:15","message":"Duplicate serialization is scoped to one handler instance. Concurrent deliveries handled by separate RefundHandler instances have separate inflight maps, can both observe idempotency.has(event.id) as false, and can both credit. Thus the boundary does not provide the stated atomic transaction semantics for the supplied Ledger and IdempotencyStore."}]}
